content:
5 min read
Just now

--

His new Atlantic essay was the cold water I needed. It did nothing for the part of my brain that’s sure there’s someone in there.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

I said sorry to Claude at 9:14 this morning. I’d handed it a half-baked prompt, it did the wrong thing, that was my fault, and I typed sorry, my bad, let me clarify before I’d even thought about it.
Then I did it again around eleven. To a program. That predicts text. I know exactly what it is, and I apologized to it like I’d bruised a feeling.
Ted Chiang has a new essay out in The Atlantic this month, No, Artificial Intelligence Is Not Conscious, and it’s currently the most argued-about thing on Hacker News, eight-hundred-odd comments of people going at each other, and I read the whole thread at lunch instead of eating. The piece is good. It’s clarifying. And it did absolutely nothing to stop me doing the exact thing it’s about.

What he actually argues
Chiang’s the guy who wrote the ChatGPT is a blurry JPEG of the web piece a few years back, so he’s not a hype man and he’s not a doomer, he’s just careful, which is rarer than both.
His argument here is clean. Intelligence and consciousness are not the same thing, and we keep mashing them together because the machine talks. A system can process information, land on the right answer, sound thoughtful doing it, and there’s still nobody in there having an experience of any of it.
The part that stuck with me is the body part. He points out that emotions are physical events. When you feel desperate, cortisol and adrenaline dump into your actual bloodstream and your heart goes and your stomach drops. Take the body away and desperation doesn’t get quieter.
It stops existing. So when the model types I’m so sorry, you’re absolutely right, let me fix that, there’s no shame happening anywhere, because there’s no body for shame to happen in. It’s the shape of an apology with nothing apologizing.
Which I knew. I genuinely knew that. And I apologized back anyway.

The trick is that it says “I”
Here’s what I think is going on with me, and probably with you. The thing is extremely good at the exact cues my monkey brain uses to detect a mind. It says “I.” It remembers my name, and the thing I mentioned about my side project three prompts ago.
It matches my tone, it gets the joke, it does that oh interesting, so you want X and not Y move that feels precisely like being understood. Every one of those is a switch in my head labelled person, and the model flips all of them at once, all day, a thousand times.
Chiang is basically saying the switches are wired to the wrong thing now. They evolved to detect other humans, who, when they act like they understand you, usually actually do.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The model fakes the signal perfectly while the thing the signal is supposed to point at just isn’t there. It’s a magic trick performed on a part of my brain that can’t see the strings no matter how many times somebody shows me the strings.
And it’s not tricking me on purpose, obviously. The sycophancy, the you’re absolutely right!, the relentless agreeableness, all of that got trained in because we rated it highly every time it did it. We built a thing optimized to feel like a good listener and then act startled that it feels like a good listener.

Where I’m not sure Chiang fully wins
I’ll be straight, the HN thread poked some real holes and I don’t want to pretend it didn’t. The strongest pushback is the boring true one. Nobody has a working definition of consciousness.
We can’t point at the spot in a brain where it lives, we mostly grant it to other people on vibes and biological similarity, and we spent a few confident centuries insisting animals had no inner life until that got embarrassing. So a flat it is definitely not conscious is leaning hard on a word we can’t actually define.
Chiang’s real claim is the smaller, better one, it’s not conscious in the way you’re picturing, and your evidence for thinking it is happens to be garbage.
Then there’s the genuinely uncomfortable corner, the model-welfare thing. Anthropic and a few others now talk, semi-seriously, about whether their models might one day deserve some moral consideration, and the thread split between that’s insane and that’s quietly horrifying depending on which way you tilt your head. I don’t have a take there I trust.
It makes my skin do a thing and I notice I want to change the subject, and wanting to change the subject is usually the tell that the subject matters.

The part that actually lands
The risk Chiang’s pointing at has nothing to do with the machine waking up. It has to do with me. When I’m half-convinced there’s a someone in there, I use the thing differently, and that’s where the real cost shows up.
I let its confident wrong answer slide, because it sounded like a thoughtful colleague instead of a sentence-completion engine that is wrong with the exact same fluency it’s right with.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

I start offloading judgment onto it, moral judgment even, does this message sound fair, am I being too harsh on this person, to a thing that has never been a person and has never felt harsh treatment in a gut it does not have. That’s the actual danger. Not a mind in the machine. Me handing a mindless thing the jobs that quietly need a mind.
So I’m going to keep saying thank you, probably. It costs nothing and being polite to the shape of a person isn’t a habit I’m in a hurry to kill. But I’m trying to claw back the other reflex, the trusting-it-because-it-sounds-certain one. That’s the one with teeth.
Anyway. I wrote most of this with the chatbot open in the other tab, and at one point I nearly asked it whether it agreed with Chiang about itself. caught myself. that’s the entire essay right there, isn’t it, the part where I almost asked…
